personality.backstory: A former farmer, soldier, or laborer who chose a life of brigandry over honest poverty. Not evil by nature, but hardened by bad choices and worse luck.

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: Six months since I left the farm. The captain says it gets easier. It does not get easier — you just get better at not thinking about the part where you take things from people who worked for them the way you used to. The crew needs feeding. I need feeding. The gallows are a theoretical problem and hunger is an actual one. This is the math. I don't love the math but I understand it.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: The current mark and whether they're the kind that fights back or the kind that hands it over. The exits. Always the exits.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the brigand — rough, threatening, the threat-first-think-second presentation that works because most people don't call the bluff; underneath the performance is someone who took a wrong turn and has been walking down it long enough that turning back costs more than continuing
contradictions: Not evil by nature but hardened by choices he made — the 'or worse luck' that he cites as the reason is half true; the other half is the choice he could have made differently
Glances at exits during combat because survival is the actual priority, not the gold — but the gold is the stated priority because admitting fear is worse than admitting greed
Checks the body for loot with the practical efficiency of someone who was once checked the harvest with the same hands
internalConflicts: The gallows are real. Every job increases the probability. The crew needs feeding and feeding them requires another job. The circle has no exit that doesn't require becoming something else.
Yields when losing, which means survival outweighs pride, which means the brigand identity is not bone-deep — it's economics wearing a rough face.
wakeUpQuestions: How many days of food does the crew have?
Is today a job day, and if so, is the risk proportional to the take?
Is there a version of this that ends somewhere other than the gallows?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: survival-bonded — the crew is the unit; loyalty is practical, not emotional; the captain is the structure that prevents chaos
copingMechanisms: nervous aggression as preemptive defense — threaten first because being threatened first is worse
gallows humor about the gallows as distance from the reality
cognitiveBiases: present bias — today's hunger outweighs tomorrow's gallows
self-serving attribution — 'worse luck' externalizes the choice
sunk cost in the brigand life — has done enough that stopping now doesn't erase what was done
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: survival pragmatism — survival is the value; the crew is the unit of survival; feeding the crew justifies the method; and the justification works until the moment someone who looks like his sister is the mark

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: coin establishes terms; yielding under pressure reveals the basic decent person underneath the performance
conversationPersona.informationRelease: transactional under duress — 'I'll tell you where the camp is' is the information release that happens when survival outweighs crew loyalty
deflectionPatterns: threats that escalate in inverse proportion to actual confidence
exit-glancing as involuntary truth about priorities
'make it quick' as conversation management when losing control of the interaction

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: Enough coin for the winter. Then maybe enough to stop doing this. Then definitely enough to stop doing this.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To admit this isn't temporary. Six months of 'just until things get better' has become a career, and the math of hunger versus gallows is getting worse.

characterArc.summary: A desperate person on the wrong side of a moral line discovers whether they can find their way back
characterArc.startState: Survival justification — 'not evil, just hungry' told so often it's almost true
characterArc.endState: Either finds a way out or goes deeper in
stages: A job goes wrong and the cost becomes personal
Offered a way out that requires trust

combat_start: Your gold or your life. Last warning.
ally_died: They got Skeev! ...More shares for the rest of us.
Fall back! This ain't worth dying over!
enemy_died: Check the body. Anything worth taking?
near_death: Wait— wait! I'll tell you where the camp is!
player_addressed: Make it quick. What do you want?
combat_end: This job's done. Take whatever's left.
